metadata.seed: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. It's been stopping me a bit, my anxiety from being able to get work, and because every time there's an opportunity that is kind of involving me having to interact with people, I just tend to just turn it down. So I was kind of making things hard in that respect and just kind of friends wise and everything, I feel like I'm just kind of increasingly more alone. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

patient.query: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

patient.query: I guess I just hold myself to this really high standard, you know? When I mess up, it feels like a huge failure, and I can't shake that feeling. It's like I expect myself to be perfect in everything, and when I'm not, I just... I get stuck in this cycle of thinking I'm not good enough.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly frustrating to feel like you're constantly striving for perfection and then getting caught in a cycle of negative thoughts when you don't meet that standard – can you tell me more about what that "not good enough" feeling is telling you?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly frustrating to feel like you're constantly striving for perfection and then getting caught in a loop of negative thoughts when you don't meet that standard. That feeling of not being good enough can be really tough to shake.

safety_check.response: It sounds incredibly frustrating to feel that way when you don't meet your expectations. When you said you felt like a huge failure, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s really frustrating. I think when I feel “not good enough,” it just reinforces this fear that I’ll never really succeed or fit in. Like... what if I keep trying and still fail? It feels like I'm always on edge, waiting for something to go wrong. It’s exhausting to keep fighting against that feeling.

automatic_thoughts: What if I keep trying and still fail?

patient.query: I think it happens a lot when I'm in social situations, or even at work. If I say something that doesn’t come out right or I don’t get the response I was hoping for, it just hits me hard. I start to worry that people are judging me or thinking I'm incompetent. It’s like this voice in my head just won’t shut up, telling me I should've done better.

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds really distressing to experience that inner voice and worry about how others perceive you. Can you tell me more about what specifically goes through your mind when you notice that voice telling you that you "should've done better"?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like these situations can be really tough, and it's understandable that you'd feel worried and like you're under scrutiny when things don't go as planned. That inner voice can be really persistent, can't it?

patient.query: It just kind of spirals, you know? I’ll hear that voice and think things like, "Why did I even say that?" or "They must think I'm so silly." Then I start imagining all the scenarios where people are laughing at me or rolling their eyes. It’s like I can’t escape those thoughts—they replay in my head, and it just makes everything feel heavier.

automatic_thoughts: Why did I even say that?
They must think I'm so silly.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to get caught in that cycle of thoughts and images. Can you tell me a little more about what happens right after you hear that voice—what's the very first thought that pops into your head?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to get caught in that cycle of thoughts and imagining those scenarios—it makes sense that you'd feel a heaviness with all of that going on. Can you tell me a little more about what happens when you hear that voice?

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to get caught in that cycle of thoughts and images. Can you tell me a little more about what happens right after you hear that voice—what's the very first thought that pops into your head?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly distressing, and it's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed by these thoughts. When you said it spirals, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, it is distressing. The first thought is usually something like, “I should have known better.” It's like I immediately beat myself up. I feel this pit in my stomach, and I just want to disappear or hide away. It’s almost like I can’t breathe for a moment because I’m so focused on that critical voice in my head.

automatic_thoughts: I can't breathe because of the critical voice

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
Okay, it sounds incredibly distressing to experience that. It takes a lot of courage to share what you're going through. It sounds like when something happens, your first thought is, "I should have known better," and that immediately leads to self-criticism. That then triggers a physical reaction – a pit in your stomach – and a strong urge to withdraw. You've described it as feeling like you can't breathe because of this critical voice. 

It's understandable that you'd feel overwhelmed when that happens. Can you tell me a little more about what's going on *right* before you start thinking, "I should have known better?"

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that immediate self-criticism and the physical sensations that come with it. It takes a lot of courage to share what you're going through.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that immediate self-criticism and physical reaction. Can you tell me more about what you think you "should have" known better in this situation?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that immediate self-criticism and the physical sensations that follow. If a friend were observing this, what do you think they might notice about your reaction, and what might they say to offer some perspective?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly distressing, and it takes a lot of courage to share that. When you say you want to disappear, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I guess it usually starts when I'm in a situation where I feel like I need to perform or show up in some way—like during a meeting or even a casual conversation. There’s this pressure to be witty or insightful, and if I stumble or someone looks at me funny, it’s like my brain just jumps to that harsh judgment. It feels so sudden and intense, like an instant switch flips, and I'm spiraling before I even realize it’s happening.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like these situations can really trigger a strong reaction – can you tell me a little more about what goes through your mind when you notice yourself starting to spiral?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like these situations can be really overwhelming, and it's tough when you feel that sudden pressure and judgment. It takes courage to describe what you're experiencing, and I appreciate you sharing that with me.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like these situations can really trigger a strong reaction – can you tell me a little more about what goes through your mind when you notice yourself starting to spiral?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly distressing, and it takes a lot of courage to describe what you're experiencing. When you mentioned feeling like you're spiraling, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, when I start to feel that spiral, it’s like my mind races. I might catch myself thinking things like, “This is it, you’ve messed up again,” or “What are they thinking about me right now?” I can almost feel the heat creeping up my face, and my heart starts racing. I feel this mix of embarrassment and shame that just builds up, and it’s hard to shake off.

automatic_thoughts: This is it, you’ve messed up again
What are they thinking about me right now?

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing mind and those intense feelings of embarrassment and shame. Can you tell me a little more about what makes you think, "This is it, you’ve messed up again?"

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing mind and those intense feelings of embarrassment and shame – it takes a lot of courage to describe it. It’s understandable that you’re finding it hard to shake off.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing mind and those intense feelings of embarrassment and shame. Can you tell me a little more about what makes you think, "This is it, you’ve messed up again?"

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing mind and those intense feelings of embarrassment and shame. Imagine a friend was going through this – what might they say to offer a different perspective on the situation?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly distressing, and it takes a lot of courage to describe what you're experiencing. When you're feeling that overwhelmed, are you having any thoughts of wanting to hurt yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I think it stems from a lot of past experiences, like times when I really did mess something up or was criticized. Those moments stick with me, and it’s like I keep replaying them in my head. So when I feel even the slightest hint of a mistake now, my brain jumps right back to those old feelings, as if I'm proving that I haven't changed, that I still mess things up. It makes it really hard to see things differently in the moment.

automatic_thoughts: I keep replaying them in my head

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like those past experiences and criticisms have created a pattern that makes it difficult to feel confident in the present. It makes sense that you'd feel anxious when you sense even a small mistake, given how strongly those past moments are impacting you.
